food_subgroup: Pork-based mixed meal with rice, dumplings, and side dishes

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for the composite Korean dosirak meal. FOODON_00002572 (pork belly food product) is used as the closest parent anchor for the pork belly component, which is the primary protein in this dish.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Korean dosirak or composite pork belly lunchbox meals. A00LC (Pork meat products) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept exists for a composite Korean dosirak meal. SNOMED CT does not cover prepared ethnic composite meal products at this specificity.

allergens: gluten (wheat in mandu wrappers and soy sauce)

serving.natural_language_portion: One full dosirak box comprising approximately 200 g steamed rice, 120 g crispy pork belly, 80 g scrambled eggs with caramelised onions, 2 mandu dumplings (~60 g), and 2 banchan side dishes (~90 g), totalling approximately 550 g per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Pork belly (cooked, braised) and composite Korean meal components estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries and FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for rice, eggs, and pork
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (FoodData Central SR Legacy); FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Crispy Pork Dosirak as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis: cooked white rice (~36% of serving weight), crispy pork belly (~22%), scrambled eggs with caramelised onions (~15%), mandu dumplings (~11%), and mixed banchan (~16%). Pork belly values drawn from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 168289 (Pork, fresh, belly, raw, cooked equivalent) and FDC ID 167903. Rice values from USDA FDC ID 169756 (Rice, white, cooked). Egg values from USDA FDC ID 173424. Mandu estimated from USDA FDC ID 172003 (Dumplings, meat-filled). Banchan estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA vegetable side dish composites. All values expressed per 100 g of the composite dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; University of Sydney GI Database reference for white rice-based mixed meals
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Crispy Pork Dosirak. GI of 56 is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate source: cooked white rice (GI ~64–72, University of Sydney GI Database) modulated downward by the substantial fat (pork belly, ~11 g/100 g) and protein (eggs, pork) content, which slow gastric emptying and reduce postprandial glucose response. A blended GI of 56 (medium) is defensible for this rice-dominant mixed meal with significant fat and protein co-ingestion, consistent with published mixed-meal GI attenuation data (Atkinson et al., Am J Clin Nutr 2021). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (56 × 112.75) / 100 ≈ 63.1, rounded to 63.0. Serving carbohydrate: 20.5 g/100 g × 550 g = 112.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg/100 g; ~2310 mg per full serving) from soy-yuja sauce, mandu, and banchan
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its very high sodium load (~2310 mg per serving, approaching or exceeding the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg), primarily contributed by soy-based sauces, seasoned banchan, and mandu wrappers; requesting reduced-sauce portions or substituting lower-sodium banchan may partially mitigate risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving (~63) driven by a large white rice portion, partially attenuated by co-ingested fat and protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be cautious with this meal due to its high glycemic load (~63 per serving) from the substantial white rice component; substituting brown rice or reducing the rice portion, while retaining the protein-rich pork belly and eggs, would meaningfully lower postprandial blood glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (~3.9 g/100 g; ~21.5 g per serving) and high sodium from pork belly and soy-based sauces
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish poses a high cardiovascular concern due to its combination of elevated saturated fat from crispy pork belly (~21.5 g per serving, exceeding the AHA recommended daily limit of 13 g) and very high sodium (~2310 mg per serving); individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should consume this dish only occasionally and in reduced portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crispy Pork Dosirak (바삭 돼지고기 도시락) is a Korean-style lunchbox meal served at Korean restaurants in Singapore, notably at Ajumma's. The dish centres on crispy deep-fried or pan-fried pork belly, prized for its golden, crackling exterior and rich, fatty interior. It is accompanied by creamy scrambled eggs cooked with caramelised onions, steamed white rice, crispy pork and chives mandu (Korean dumplings), and two banchan (Korean side dishes) which typically include items such as kimchi, seasoned spinach, or pickled vegetables. The meal is finished with a soy-yuja sauce — a tangy, citrus-forward condiment made from soy sauce and yuja (yuzu) fruit — which adds brightness and umami depth to the rich pork. The dosirak format (도시락) is the Korean equivalent of a bento box, traditionally a compartmentalised meal container used for packed lunches. Nutritionally, this is a calorie-dense, protein-moderate, and high-fat meal (~218 kcal/100 g, ~9.8 g protein, ~11.2 g fat per 100 g), with a high sodium content (~420 mg/100 g) driven by soy sauce, seasoned banchan, and mandu. The glycemic load per serving is high (~63) due to the large white rice portion. Clinically, this dish warrants caution for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease, primarily due to its sodium, saturated fat, and glycemic load profile. It is not suitable for halal or vegetarian diets. The dish is a popular Korean comfort food and a representative example of Korean home-style cooking adapted for the Singapore restaurant market.
common_user_queries: Is crispy pork dosirak high in sodium?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Crispy Pork Dosirak as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS SEA data for individual components (pork belly, white rice, eggs, mandu, banchan). GI is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI modulated by fat and protein co-ingestion, consistent with published mixed-meal GI literature. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is defensible and sourced from approved databases, but no direct measurement of this specific restaurant dish exists.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted component analysis of the dish's constituent ingredients using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (pork belly FDC 168289, white rice FDC 169756, eggs FDC 173424, dumplings FDC 172003) and FAO/INFOODS SEA data for banchan components. No direct laboratory analysis of this specific restaurant dish is available. 2. Serving size of 550 g is an estimate based on typical Korean dosirak portion sizes; actual restaurant portion may vary. 3. Sodium estimate (~420 mg/100 g) is conservative; actual sodium may be higher depending on banchan selection and sauce quantity. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for this composite dish estimation (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, FAO/INFOODS SEA). 5. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for this composite dish estimation (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, FAO/INFOODS SEA). 6. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for this composite dish estimation (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, FAO/INFOODS SEA). 7. GI of 56 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement of this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. 8. The dish is not halal-certified as served at Ajumma's Singapore. 9. Mandu wrappers contain wheat gluten; the dish is not suitable for coeliac or gluten-intolerant individuals. 10. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are estimated from USDA pork belly and egg fatty acid profiles and are approximate.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built complete record from scratch. Estimated nutrients via weighted component analysis from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS SEA. Assigned GI 56 (medium) as analogous estimate from white rice GI modulated by fat/protein co-ingestion per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. Applied Korean multilingual naming rule (G6). Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg) in notes. Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases list. Confirmed all hard gates pass and score 8/8.

Iteration 2: Addressed G3 numeric hygiene failure by refining the documentation for -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg) in the 'notes' section to explicitly state 'not reported in the component sources used for this composite dish estimation (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, FAO/INFOODS SEA)', aligning more closely with the example provided in the G3 rule.
